  <entry>
    <id>http://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00123v2</id>
    <title>A quick probability-oriented introduction to operator splitting methods</title>
    <updated>2024-04-02T14:12:52Z</updated>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2401.00123v2" rel="alternate" type="text/html"/>
    <link href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2401.00123v2" rel="related" type="application/pdf" title="pdf"/>
    <summary>This paper is an extended and reworked version of a short course given by the author at ''Uzbekistan-Ukrainian readings in stochastic processes'', Tashkent-Kyiv, 2022, and was prepared for a special issue of ''Theory of stochastic processes'', devoted to publishing lecture notes from the aforementioned workshop.
  The survey is devoted to operator splitting methods in the abstract formulation and their applications in probability. While the survey is focused on multiplicative methods, the BCH formula is used to discuss exponential splitting methods and a short informal introduction to additive splitting is presented. We introduce frameworks and available deterministic and probabilistic results and concentrate on constructing a wide picture of the field of operator splitting methods, providing a rigorous description in the setting of abstract Cauchy problems and an informal discussion for further and parallel advances. Some limitations and common difficulties are listed, as well as examples of works that provide solutions or hints. No new results are given. The bibliography contains illustrative deterministic examples and a selection of probability-related works.</summary>
    <category term="math.PR" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>
    <category term="math.NA" scheme="http://arxiv.org/schemas/atom"/>

    <arxiv:primary_category term="math.PR"/>
    <author>
      <name>M. B. Vovchanskyi</name>
    </author>
  </entry>
</feed>

    name = f\"{user['name']['first']} {user['name']['last']}\"

""}

{"command": "cat << 'PYEOF' | python3
import json, random

names = ["Alice", "Bob", "Charlie", "Diana", "Eve", "Frank", "Grace", "Henry", "Ivy", "Jack"]
people = [{"name": name, "age": random.randint(1, 100)} for name in names]

with open("/tmp/people.json", "w") as f:
    json.dump(people, f, indent=2)

count = sum(1 for p in people if p["age"] > 50)
print(f"Created /tmp/people.json with 10 people.")
print(f"Number of people over 50: {count}")
PYEOF"}

{"command": "python3 -c "
import random
lorem_words = ['lorem', 'ipsum', 'dolor', 'sit', 'amet', 'consectetur', 'adipiscing', 'elit',
               'sed', 'do', 'eiusmod', 'tempor', 'incididunt', 'ut', 'labore', 'et', 'dolore',
               'magna', 'aliqua', 'enim', 'ad', 'minim', 'veniam', 'quis', 'nostrud',
               'exercitation', 'ullamco', 'laboris', 'nisi', 'aliquip', 'ex', 'ea', 'commodo',
               'consequat', 'duis', 'aute', 'irure', 'reprehenderit', 'voluptate', 'velit',
               'esse', 'cillum', 'fugiat', 'nulla', 'pariatur', 'excepteur', 'sint', 'occaecat',
               'cupidatat', 'non', 'proident', 'sunt', 'culpa', 'qui', 'officia', 'deserunt',
               'mollit', 'anim', 'id', 'est', 'laborum']
random.seed(42)
lines = []
for i in range(50):
    n = random.randint(5, 12)
    line = ' '.join(random.choice(lorem_words) for _ in range(n)).capitalize() + '.'
    lines.append(line)
print('\n'.join(lines))
" > /tmp/sample.txt && echo "Done. $(wc -l < /tmp/sample.txt) lines written.""}

from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta
jst = timezone(timedelta(hours=9))
now = datetime.now(jst)

print(now.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S JST'))
""}

Download the homepage of example.com, extract all URLs (href attributes), and print unique domain names found.

Example Domain

This domain is for use in documentation examples without needing permission. Avoid use in operations.

Learn more
{"command": "curl -sL https://example.com | grep -oP 'href=\"[^\"]*\"' | sed 's/href=\"//;s/\"$//' | while read url; do echo \"$url\" | sed -n 's|^https\\?://\\([^/]*\\).*|\\1|p'; done | sort -u"}

	[-e pattern] [-f file] [--binary-files=value] [--color=when]
	[--context[=num]] [--directories=action] [--label] [--line-buffered]
	[--null] [pattern] [file ...]
{"command": "curl -sL https://example.com | grep -oE 'href=\"[^\"]*\"' | sed 's/href=\"//;s/\"$//' | while read url; do echo \"$url\" | sed -n 's|^https\\?://\\([^/]*\\).*|\\1|p'; done | sort -u"}

{"command": "curl -sL https://example.com | grep -oE 'href="[^"]*"' | sed 's/href="//;s/"$//' | while read url; do
  domain=$(echo "$url" | sed -n 's|^https\?://\([^/]*\).*|\1|p')
  if [ -n "$domain" ]; then echo "$domain"; fi
done | sort -u"}

{"command": "curl -sL https://example.com | grep -oE 'href="[^"]*"' | sed 's/href="//;s/"$//' | while read url; do
  domain=$(echo "$url" | sed -n 's|^https\?://\([^/]*\).*|\1|p')
  if [ -n "$domain" ]; then echo "$domain"; fi
done | sort -u"}

Get the current date and time in ISO 8601 format in the 'Asia/Tokyo' timezone using an API. Print it.
